Q1: I tried to keep a consistent routine, studying around 27 hours each week spread across most days. I usually blocked out time in the mornings before my part-time work shifts, and then again in the evenings. With limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when I could access reliable internet at the library or campus. I kept a simple planner where I wrote down what needed to be done each week, though sometimes life got in the way of sticking to it perfectly.
Q2: My pattern was mostly regular, but it definitely shifted when deadlines approached. I tried to maintain those morning and evening study blocks, but if an assignment was due, I'd push harder in the days leading up to it. Sometimes work would demand extra hours and I'd have to compress my studying into fewer days. Being 24 and having more responsibilities than some younger students meant I had to be flexible even when I preferred routine.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS to check for new announcements or to see if any assignments had been posted or updated. Sometimes it was just to download lecture notes or readings so I could study them offline later. The gamified tasks they introduced actually got me logging in more often to check my progress and see if I'd earned any points or badges. Fear of missing something important was honestly a big motivator for me.
Q4: After a few weeks, checking the LMS in the morning became automatic, like checking my email. The gamified elements helped make it feel less like a chore and more like something I could track progress on. I'd log in, see what tasks were available, and plan my day around completing them. It wasn't exciting exactly, but it became a habit I could rely on even when my motivation was low.
Q5: The reading materials and written lecture notes were most useful to me since I learn best through reading and writing. I'd download PDFs and annotate them heavily, making my own summaries. The discussion forums were also helpful when I could see how other students interpreted difficult concepts. The gamified tasks gave me structure and small goals to hit, which helped when I was feeling scared about whether I was keeping up.
Q6: I often skipped the video content or only skimmed through it quickly. With my internet situation being unreliable at home, streaming videos was frustrating and time-consuming. I also didn't engage much with peer interactions beyond what was necessary because I felt like I didn't have the time or energy for extensive collaboration. Some optional readings I just couldn't get to given everything else on my plate.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams. If I could see how it would help my grade or deepen my understanding of something I'd be tested on, I'd prioritize it. The gamified elements helped too because earning points made even smaller tasks feel like accomplishments. Honestly, with limited time and resources, I had to be ruthless about what I invested energy in.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford. Required ones had clear consequences if I didn't do them, so they automatically got priority. With optional stuff, I'd think about whether the effort would actually pay off or if my time was better spent on something graded. Sometimes I felt guilty about skipping optional things, but I had to be practical about my situation.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download all the readings and notes as soon as they were posted, then work through them on my laptop without internet. I filled notebooks with summaries and practice answers written out by hand. I also used some textbooks from the library that weren't part of the official course materials but helped me understand concepts better.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes and rewriting key concepts, but none of that showed up anywhere in the system. I'd also quiz myself using flashcards I made, or work through practice problems on paper. Sometimes I'd discuss course material with a friend over coffee, working through difficult ideas together. The LMS only saw me when I was online, not when I was grinding through material at my desk.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach actually. I'd access a reading or lecture note once to download it, then I'd study it extensively offline over several days. My internet access wasn't reliable enough to keep going back to the LMS, so I learned to grab everything I needed in one session. The system probably showed minimal engagement, but I was actually working through that material thoroughly.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods, especially mid-semester when work picked up extra shifts and assignments started piling up. I felt scared about falling behind and that fear sometimes paralyzed me rather than motivating me. The stress was real, sitting at a medium level constantly, making it hard to focus even when I had time. When multiple deadlines converged, I'd feel overwhelmed and just shut down for a day or two.
Q13: Fatigue was a big factor for me. Working part-time while studying full-time at 24 meant I was often tired when I finally sat down to engage with course material. Sometimes I'd log into the LMS intending to participate in discussions but just wouldn't have the mental energy to contribute meaningfully. Competing responsibilities definitely meant the LMS got less of my attention than it probably should have.
Q14: There were times I'd scroll through discussion posts or skim readings without really absorbing anything. I'd be logged in, technically active, but my mind was elsewhere worrying about bills or work schedules. Sometimes I'd watch the progress bar on a video without actually paying attention to the content. It felt like going through the motions to check boxes rather than genuinely learning.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts more than I should have because they felt less urgent than written assignments. Even though I knew participation mattered, the immediate pressure of a graded assignment always won out. Sometimes I avoided starting big assignments because I was scared of not doing well enough, which only made the fear worse. It's a cycle I recognized but struggled to break.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my LMS usage patterns. I'd be more active in the days leading up to due dates, downloading materials and checking requirements multiple times. The gamified system helped spread things out a bit because I could earn points for completing tasks early. But honestly, without a deadline looming, my engagement dropped significantly because other life demands took over.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement, though not always positively. Good feedback motivated me to keep going and showed me I was on the right track. But when feedback was delayed or vague, I felt uncertain about whether my approach was working. Sometimes waiting for feedback made me anxious about starting the next assignment because I didn't know if I needed to change my methods.
Q18: The course structure with its gamified elements gave me clear milestones to work toward, which helped with my low motivation. Having tasks broken into smaller pieces made the workload feel more manageable. But the structure also assumed consistent internet access and time availability that I didn't always have. Weekly modules worked okay for me, though I sometimes fell a week behind and had to catch up in bursts.
Q19: The gamification actually made engagement easier because it gave me small wins to celebrate even when I was struggling. Being able to see my progress visually helped combat the fear that I wasn't doing enough. However, the heavy reliance on video content made things harder given my internet situation. More downloadable text-based resources would have suited my learning style and circumstances better.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for information or trying to understand requirements without actually completing work. They could be anxious about assignments and keep checking without submitting. Or maybe they're dealing with personal issues that make it hard to follow through even when they're trying to engage. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity, especially when someone is stressed or confused.
Q21: This definitely reflects my experience to some degree. I performed reasonably well but my LMS activity was lower than it could have been because so much of my studying happened offline. Students with good study skills can be efficient with their online time and do the heavy lifting elsewhere. The LMS doesn't capture reading physical notes, working through problems on paper, or discussing concepts with peers in person.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in, how often, and what they click on. They can track submission times and participation frequency. But they miss the quality of engagement, whether someone is actually thinking deeply or just scrolling. They can't see offline studying, emotional state, or the real effort behind each interaction. Numbers don't capture understanding.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material and trying to connect it to what I already know. It's not just completing tasks but caring about whether I understand them. For me, engagement includes the worry and effort I put in even when it doesn't show up in any system. It's showing up mentally even when I'm scared about my performance or stressed about other parts of life.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much time I spent with downloaded materials, rewriting notes, or studying from textbooks. It wouldn't capture the fear I felt about falling behind or the resilience it took to keep going despite low motivation and limited resources. My peer discussions happened mostly offline, and my deepest learning often came from quiet reflection that no system could measure. The emotional labor of being a student with competing responsibilities is completely invisible in the data.
